coredata.dc:title: Comparative investigation on carbon dots and chloride fluxes modified ZnAl2O4:Cr3+ nanophosphors for temperature sensing, cutting-edge forensic, anti-counterfeiting and w-LEDs applications

coredata.dc:description:
A novel Cr3+ doped ZnAl2O4 is synthesized by grafting carbon dots (CDs) and chloride fluxes (NaCl, KCl, NH4Cl) through the solid-state reaction method. Upon excitation at 530 nm wavelength, the Cr3+ ions in the ZAO NPs emit red light due to the influence of a strong crystal field. This red emission arises from the electric dipole 2Eg→4A2 g of the Cr3+ ions. Among the chloride fluxes examined, NH4Cl had a significant impact on the PL intensity. Notably, when CDs are incorporated into the ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs, a remarkable increase of 17.25-folds in photoluminescence (PL) intensity is observed. This enhancement is substantially higher than that of CDs alone (11.23-folds) and NH4Cl alone (3.82-folds). The increased PL intensity is attributed to the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. Furthermore, the addition of CDs (5 wt.%) enhanced the colour purity (CP) of ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs to 99.8%, achieving a high Internal quantum efficiency (I
QE ) of 93.4 %. Notably, the CDs (5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs maintained 92.43 % of their emission intensity even at 420 K, demonstrating exceptional thermal stability compared to ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs. Moreover, the NPs holds promise for optical thermometry, boasting a high relative sensitivity (Sr) of 6.74 × 10–2 %K-1 across a temperature range spanning from 300 to 480 K. Moreover, the latent fingerprints (LFPs) developed using CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs demonstrate remarkable selectivity and high contrast. Under UV irradiation, the structural features of LFPs at levels I–III are clearly visible. In addition, the strong red fluorescence emitted by CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs makes them suitable for applications in AC. CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs exhibit significant potential for w-LED fabrication, achieving a correlated colour temperature (CCT) of 5517 K, a CIE coordinate of (0.332, 0.331), and a high colour rendering index (CRI) of Ra = 94, outperforming ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs. Overall results clearly demonstrates that, CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs are effective for applications in optical thermometry, dactyloscopy, w-LEDs, and AC.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A novel Cr3+ doped ZnAl2O4 is synthesized by grafting carbon dots (CDs) and chloride fluxes (NaCl, KCl, NH4Cl) through the solid-state reaction method. Upon excitation at 530 nm wavelength, the Cr3+ ions in the ZAO NPs emit red light due to the influence of a strong crystal field. This red emission arises from the electric dipole 2Eg→4A2 g of the Cr3+ ions. Among the chloride fluxes examined, NH4Cl had a significant impact on the PL intensity. Notably, when CDs are incorporated into the ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs, a remarkable increase of 17.25-folds in photoluminescence (PL) intensity is observed. This enhancement is substantially higher than that of CDs alone (11.23-folds) and NH4Cl alone (3.82-folds). The increased PL intensity is attributed to the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. Furthermore, the addition of CDs (5 wt.%) enhanced the colour purity (CP) of ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs to 99.8%, achieving a high Internal quantum efficiency (I
QE ) of 93.4 %. Notably, the CDs (5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs maintained 92.43 % of their emission intensity even at 420 K, demonstrating exceptional thermal stability compared to ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs. Moreover, the NPs holds promise for optical thermometry, boasting a high relative sensitivity (Sr) of 6.74 × 10–2 %K-1 across a temperature range spanning from 300 to 480 K. Moreover, the latent fingerprints (LFPs) developed using CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs demonstrate remarkable selectivity and high contrast. Under UV irradiation, the structural features of LFPs at levels I–III are clearly visible. In addition, the strong red fluorescence emitted by CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs makes them suitable for applications in AC. CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs exhibit significant potential for w-LED fabrication, achieving a correlated colour temperature (CCT) of 5517 K, a CIE coordinate of (0.332, 0.331), and a high colour rendering index (CRI) of Ra = 94, outperforming ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs. Overall results clearly demonstrates that, CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs are effective for applications in optical thermometry, dactyloscopy, w-LEDs, and AC.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The Cr3+ ion stands out as one of the pioneering transition metal ions utilized in luminescence temperature assessments and remains the most extensively employed among all transition metal ions. In the expanding realm of biological research, transition metal ions such as Ti2+, Co2+, Ni2+, V2+ and Cr3+ are being harnessed for temperature measurements in tissues and cells across all three biological windows [1]. Luminescence (nano)thermometry technique, which is based on non-invasive, remote detection of temperature based on monitoring the temperature-induced changes of some luminescence features of optically active probes (luminescent materials), allowing temperature monitoring in microscopic and nano-sized areas. In other words, this technique utilizes temperature-dependent steady-state or time-resolved spectroscopic features for remote temperature sensing, such as luminescence/fluorescence intensity ratio (LIR/FIR), signal intensity, spectral position of the emission line, full-width-at-half maximum (FWHM) of the band, or luminescence decay/rise times, respectively. Each of these factors can be used as a thermometric parameter, whose change can be correlated and calibrated with temperature of the system.

Scientists are focused on improving the luminescent properties of NPs doped with transition metal ions, aiming to enhance emission characteristics and tailor the optical behaviour of these materials for various applications. The effects of various fluxes and their applications are outlined in Table S1 [14–22]. A study by A. K. Vishwakarma et al. [15] explored the characteristics of different chloride fluxes (NH4Cl, KCl, LiCl, NaCl) when combined with Cr3+ doped BaNb2O6, synthesized using the solid-state reaction method, aiming to create an intense red light-emitting phosphor. Luminescent studies revealed that NH4Cl flux is the most suitable among the chloride fluxes for the BaNb2O6 phase, enhancing intensity by two-fold without altering band position or crystal structure. Another investigation by Kaur et al. [21] focused on synthesizing a novel phosphor, La5Si3O12N:Ce3+, using NH4Cl as the flux through solid-state synthesis. Experimental findings demonstrated that the flux boosted the PL intensity by 1.48 folds. Similarly, Zhang et al. [22] examined the impact of chloride fluxes on Sr0.8Ca0.19AlSiN3:Cr2+ NPs, concluding NH4Cl as the most suitable flux for the developed NPs. However, the selection of fluxes often relies on trial and error, lacking a general rule for choosing specific fluxes. As far as we know, there are no research reports detailing the specific effects of chloride fluxes or CDs on enhancing the PL of Cr3+-doped ZAO nanoparticles (NPs).

The integration of CDs with functional molecules and materials has garnered attention for overcoming NPs limitations and expanding applications. CDs readily form composites due to their surface groups, improving properties when combined with polymers by reducing disadvantages and enhancing benefits. Their small size and surface chemistry promote strong interactions and uniform dispersion in nanoparticles. These composites offer high optical absorptivity, sensitivity, chemical stability, low toxicity, and thermal responsiveness, while CDs/polymer composites enhance flexibility, strength, durability, and hydrophobicity [23]. Applications span AC, UV shielding, optoelectronics, super-capacitors, catalysts, and sensors [24–26]. CDs are increasingly used for visualizing latent fingerprints due to their strong fluorescence and large surface area. CDs bind to fingerprint residues, making prints highly visible under UV or visible light. Their tunable emission properties enhance contrast and detection against various backgrounds. CDs can be combined with other techniques to improve the visibility of fine ridge details and minutiae, forming stable, bright luminescent complexes that enhance the clarity and reliability of latent fingerprint images [2–6].

CDs are used in anti-counterfeiting due to their PL and size-tunable emission. Incorporated into inks or coatings, CDs create luminescent, multi-coloured patterns that are difficult to replicate. Their fluorescence can be tuned to specific wavelengths, enabling unique markers. CDs also enhance hidden security features visible under UV light, aiding in counterfeit detection. Their small size and biocompatibility allow seamless integration into various substrates without altering physical properties [7–10]. CDs enhance colour rendering and stability in w-LEDs. Integrated into the phosphor layer or blend, CDs improve white light quality by providing better colour consistency and a wider colour gamut. Their PL helps convert blue LED light into a fuller white spectrum, while also increasing LED efficiency and lifespan by reducing phosphor degradation. CDs contribute to warmer colour temperatures and better light distribution, resulting in energy-efficient and visually appealing lighting [11–13].

In this study, we synthesized Cr³⁺-doped ZAO using NH₄Cl, KCl and NaCl as fluxes through the solid-state reaction method to investigate the influence of chloride fluxes. Additionally, we fabricated CDs-grafted Cr³⁺-doped ZAO NPs to compare the effects of CDs and fluxes on the NPs. We conducted comparative studies on the effects of fluxes and CDs on the structural, morphological, and optical properties of NPs. Furthermore, some of the synthesized NPs are utilized for powder dusting of FPs and palm prints. The samples are then applied in optical thermometry, LFP development, w-LEDs, and AC applications.

Diammonium hydrogen citrate, urea, Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), Aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO3)3·9H2O), Chromium(III) nitrate nonahydrate Cr(NO3)3·9H2O (99 %), Ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), sodium chloride (NaCl) and potassium chloride (KCl). These chemicals used are of analytical grade and had a purity of 99.9 % (Sigma Aldrich). Deionized water served as the solvent for all experiments.

In a typical synthesis procedure, 0.2 gs of diammonium hydrogen citrate and 0.2 g of urea are carefully combined and thoroughly ground together using an agate mortar to ensure a homogeneous mixture. This fine mixture is then transferred to an oven, where it is heated at 180 °C for 3 h under ambient atmospheric conditions, allowing the reaction to proceed. After the heating process is complete, the resulting product is dissolved in water to create a solution. To remove any large particles that may be present, this solution is subjected to high-speed centrifugation at 12,000 revolutions per minute (rpm) for 5 min. The supernatant obtained from the centrifugation is then further purified by passing it through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to isolate the CDs. Following filtration, the solution containing the CDs is subjected to a lyophilization process, also known as freeze-drying, to convert the CDs into a solid form. The solid CDs are then dispersed in deionized water for subsequent characterization and analysis. Fig. 1
a provides a visual representation of the entire synthesis process for the CDs.

A series of undoped and ZAO:Cr3+ (1–11 mol%) NPs are synthesized via the conventional solid-state reaction method. Initially, stoichiometric quantities of Zinc nitrate, Aluminium nitrate and chromium nitrate are mixed thoroughly in a dispersing medium (acetone) and grinded in an agate mortar for an hour. After adequate grinding, the samples are sintered at 1200 °C with a controlled heating rate over 5 h and subsequently cooled to room temperature naturally in a programmable muffle furnace. The obtained powder samples are ground again for various characterizations. Fig. 1 b depicts the schematic representation of the solid-state reaction synthesis process for both undoped and Cr3+-doped ZAO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of flux assisted ZnAl2O4: Cr3+ NCs
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare ZAO:7Cr3+/fluxes nanoparticles, the synthesis process is identical to that of ZAO:Cr3+ nanoparticles, with the addition of NH4Cl, NaCl, and KCl as fluxes during grinding. Each flux is added in an amount equivalent to 1.5 % of the total weight. Fig. 1 b illustrates the schematic representation of the synthesis process for flux-assisted ZAO:7Cr3+ nanoparticles.

To prepare CDs@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs, the CDs solutions of different concentration (1, 3, 5 and 7 wt.%) are added to ZAO:7Cr3+ (0.5 g) and sonicated at room temperature for 15 min until a uniform gel formed. The resulting gel is then dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h Subsequently, the xerogel is ground into a powder to yield the final CDs@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs. Fig. 1 c illustrates the synthesis process of CDs@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs. Similarly, CDs are incorporated into ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl fluxes to synthesize CDs@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs.

A variety of substrates including glass plate, stainless steel plate, marble, leaf, and aluminium foil are chosen for collecting FP samples. Two donors, one female and one male, both approximately 25 years old, washed their hands with soap and water and then air-dried them. LFPs are deposited on the different substrates by pressing their fingertips against their foreheads. The visualization of LFPs on these substrates is carried out using the powder dusting method. To obtain aged LFPs, the female donors FP samples are stored at room temperature for varying durations (30, 90, 270 days) while maintaining temperatures between 29 and 38 °C and environmental humidity levels between 50 and 80 %. These FP samples are protected from light exposure during storage.

To prepare the luminescent ink, 2 g of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:Cr3+/NH4Cl phosphor powder is combined with 10 mL of poly-vinyl chloride (PVC) solution. Next, the mixture is sonicated for 10 min using a probe sonicator to achieve a uniformly dispersed luminescent ink. Significantly, the PVC solution functions both as a solvent and a binder, ensuring superb adhesion and even dispersion of the phosphor powder. These characteristics make it ideal for formulating luminescent security ink, which has been demonstrated for practical AC applications [27].

Crafting a w-LED involves combining the UV chip (λ = 408 nm) with the red phosphor, along with commercially available green ((Ba,Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+) and blue (BaMgAl10O17:Eu2+) NPs, in a weight ratio of 8:1:3, respectively.

The phase formation of the NPs is examined by analysing powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns by a Shimadzu7000 XRD instrument equipped with CuKα radiation (λ = 0.1540 nm). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Hitachi H-8100) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are utilized to observe the synthesized samples. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy is conducted using a PerkinElmer spectrometer to analyze molecular bonds. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis is performed with the Axis Supra instrument (Kratos) using aluminium Al Kα (hν = 1486.7 eV) as the X-ray source. The Horiba Spectrofluorometer (Fluorolog-3) is used to record both excitation and emission PL spectra for the NPs mentioned.

Fig. 2
a displays the XRD pattern of un-doped and Cr3+ doped ZAO. In the synthesized ZAO NPs, peaks are observed at angles of 31.3 °, 36.9 °, 44.7 °, 49.1 °, 55.7 °, 59.4 °, 65.3 °, 74.2 ° and 77.8 °, corresponding to the (220), (311), (400), (331), (422), (511), (440), (620), and (533) crystal planes of the ZAO cubic spinel phase. This phase is identified by a Fd-3m (227) space group matched well with JCPDS-01–074–1138 [28]. The presence of sharp diffraction peaks in the graphical result indicates good crystallinity. Fig. 2 a1 provides an enlarged PXRD pattern of peak (311), revealing a lower angle shift for all ZAO:Cr3+ samples. This suggests that the lattice parameters are slightly larger than those of undoped NPs. To delve deeper into the substitution mechanism of Cr3+ in ZAO NPs, we calculated the radius percentage difference (∆r) using the following equation [29]:

The notation CN=6 denotes the coordination number, where Rd and Rs refer to the radii of the dopant and host ions, respectively. The ionic radius of the cations are as follows: Al3+ (Rs = 0.535 Å), Zn2+ (Rd = 0.74 Å), and the doped ion Cr3+ (Rd = 0.615 Å). The calculated ∆r values are 14.95 % for the pair Al3+ and Cr3+, and 16.89 % for the pair Zn2+ and Cr3+. The smaller ∆r in the case of Al3+ compared to Zn2+ suggests that Cr3+ ions occupy the position of the Al3+ ion [30]. This is because the closer match in ionic radius between Cr3+ and Al3+ facilitates substitution, especially since both ions are in the trivalent oxidation state.

The XRD patterns of ZAO:7Cr3+ samples enhanced with chloride fluxes (NaCl, KCl, NH4Cl) are presented in Fig. 2 b. It is observed that the ZAO:7Cr3+ samples treated with chloride fluxes exhibited no impurity phase, and all diffraction peaks matched those in the reference data (JCPDS-01–074–1138). The ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs are effectively modified by the addition of fluxes without affecting the phase. These results suggest that the incorporation of NaCl, KCl, and NH4Cl improved the crystallinity of ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs. In the case of CDs@ZAO:7Cr3+and CDs@ZAO:7Cr3+ /NH4Cl NCs, a noticeable hump appeared at 2θ = 20⁰ and 40⁰, which can be attributed to the CDs (Fig. 2 c) [31]. Similarly, the XRD analysis of the synthesized CDs revealed humps at 2θ values of 20 ⁰ and 40 ⁰, corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of graphitic carbon.

The Rietveld refinement technique, implemented using the GFourier tool equipped with FullProf program, is employed to extract precise structural parameters from the observed X-ray diffraction (XRD) profiles [32]. These parameters encompass factors like the scale factor, lattice parameters, pseudo-Voigt profile parameter, among others. By employing Rietveld refinement, a comprehensive comprehension of the crystal structure of ZAO materials is attained, offering valuable insights into their atomic arrangement and overall structural characteristics. Fig. 2 (d–f) illustrate the refined profiles of ZAO, ZAO:7Cr3+, and ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl nanoparticles, while the refined patterns of other samples are presented in Fig. S1(a–g). The successful fitting of the Rietveld profile further validates the phase purity of the ZAO material. Detailed refined structural parameters of the prepared materials and associated reliability indicators assessing the refinement quality are provided in Table 1 . It is noteworthy that the reliability values obtained from the analysis are within an acceptable range, emphasizing the robustness and accuracy of the results obtained. Using Rietveld refinement data, the crystal structure of ZAO is visualized using Vesta software. Fig. 2 g presents a schematic diagram of the crystal structure of ZAO, illustrating the arrangement of [ZnO4] tetrahedra and [AlO6] octahedra. Based on the refined XRD data and ion coordination information, it is observed that Cr3+ ions tend to preferentially occupy the [AlO6] octahedral sites, leading to the emission of deep red light. The [AlO6] octahedra exhibit trigonal symmetry D3d, with the third-order axis coinciding with the (−1–11) direction in the crystal lattice. As a result, Cr3+ ions located at these octahedral sites encounter a trigonal crystal field, leading to the partial splitting of their electronic states degeneracy. This phenomenon contributes to the specific optical properties, such as the emission of deep red light, associated with Cr3+-doped ZAO materials [33].

Scherrer's formula is fundamental for estimating crystallite size in X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. However, in nanocrystals, XRD spectra broadening can be influenced by both intrinsic and extrinsic factors, including crystallite size and non-intrinsic strain effects. Several methods, including Size-Strain Plot (SSP), Williamson-Hall (WH), and Halder-Wagner (HW) methods, are employed to determine crystallite size in addition to Scherrer's (SM) method. These methods provide complementary insights into the dimensions of crystallite size, considering various physical and instrumental factors affecting XRD spectra. As a result, employing multiple approaches ensures a more comprehensive and accurate estimation of crystallite size, critical for various applications requiring precise characterization of NPs. Scherrer's formula is based on [34,35]:
(2)

βm represents the calculated broadening, which includes contributions from both instrumental broadening (βi) and the broadening due to crystal size (βd). However, when measuring the β to determine the physical and instrumental broadening of the sample, we can calculate the average crystal size using the Eq. (3) [36].
(3)

β C o s θ

Here, D specifies the crystallite size, K is the shape constant (K is equal to 0.9), wavelength (λ= 1.54056 Å) for CuKα radiation, β is the FWHM, and the diffraction angle is θ. The Williamson-Hall (WH) approach is valuable for analysing intrinsic strain in NPs, considering factors such as dislocations, grain boundaries, point defects, and stacking. Unlike Scherrer's Equation, which primarily addresses the impact of crystallite size on XRD reflection, the WH approach accounts for strain-induced peak widening. By estimating intrinsic strain from the observed broadening of XRD peaks, this approach provides a more accurate calculation of crystallite size, particularly when dealing with samples exhibiting multiple reflected peaks. Overall, the broadening observed in XRD spectra can be described by the following equation, incorporating contributions from both crystallite size and intrinsic strain [37]:

Indeed, the intrinsic strain can be derived from the slope (m), while the crystallite size can be obtained from the intercept (c) from above linear Eq. (5). This linear relationship allows for a straightforward interpretation of the Williamson-Hall plot, facilitating the extraction of both intrinsic strain and crystallite size information from XRD data (Fig. S2a).

The Size-Strain (SS) plot is a technique used to analyze X-ray diffraction (XRD) peak broadening, incorporating contributions from both Gaussian and Lorentzian functions. This model uses data from lower diffraction angles to address potential inaccuracies and significant overlaps of peaks that can occur with higher-order diffraction planes. By incorporating both strain and crystallite size contributions, the SS Plot method provides a comprehensive analysis of XRD peak broadening in nanocrystalline materials. So, the SSP includes the summation of broadening generated by Lorentzian (βL) and Gaussian (βG) functions [39]:

The Gaussian function in the SSP technique represents tension expansion, whereas the Lorentzian function in the XRD pattern represents size widening. On the other hand, the XRD peak region shows a strong tail collapse and fits well with the Gaussian function. On the other hand, while the XRD peak itself does not fit the Lorentzian function, the profile's bottoms do [40]. Through the combination of both functions, the Voigt function offers a more realistic depiction of XRD peaks by concurrently taking into consideration the effects of size widening and tension expansion. This means that Eq. (8), which represents the complete width at half maximum, can be used to express the Voigt function [41]:

The change in crystallite size determined using various methods with fluxes and concentrations of Cr3+ is displayed in Fig. S2d. The crystallite size decreases when Cr3+ concentration rises, which may be related to Cr3+ larger ionic radius. The amount of crystallinity increases with the addition of flux.

The Survey spectra depicted in Fig. 3
a confirm the presence of constituent elements in the samples. The structural analysis of ZAO:7Cr3+, ZAO:7Cr3+ /NH4Cl and CDs@ ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs is conducted using XPS are shown in Fig. S3. Specifically, the XPS of the Zn peak exhibit Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2 peaks at 1044.7 and 1021.6 eV, respectively, for ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs. There is minimal variation observed in the binding energy of Zn 2p peaks in ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ /NH4Cl NCs (Fig. 3b). The deconvoluted spectra of Al 2p show two peaks at 73.90 eV and 75.13 eV, corresponding to oxides and hydroxides of aluminium. Similar peaks are observed in ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl, CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs, with binding energies of 74.52 eV, 75.19 eV, 73.8 eV, 75.1 eV, 75 eV and, 74 eV respectively [42,43] ( Fig. 3 c). The O1 s band splits into two peaks at 531 eV and 531.9 eV for ZAO:7Cr3+, and 531 eV, 532.4 eV for ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs. For CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs, the peaks are found at 531.02 eV and 532.8 eV. The peak at 531.02 eV and 531 eV corresponds to the formation of Al2O3, while peaks at 532.8 eV, 531.9 eV, and 532.4 eV correspond to other oxides formed [44]. Similarly in CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs the peaks were observed at 531.1 and 532.5 eV corresponding to the oxides formed ( Fig. 3 d).The Cr 2p XPS spectra of ZAO:7Cr3+ exhibit two peaks at 587.1 eV and 577.5 eV, corresponding to Cr 2p1/2 and Cr 2p3/2, respectively, with a binding energy difference of 8.6 eV. For CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs, the peaks are found at 587.2 eV and 578.5 eV, accredited to Cr 2p1/2 and Cr 2p3/2, respectively [45]. For CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ /NH4Cl NCs, the peaks are found at 587.7 eV and 578.9 eV, accredited to Cr 2p1/2 and Cr 2p3/2, respectively ( Fig. 3 e). The N 1 s peak at 400.2 eV observed in ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs. The N1 s peak of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ /NH4Cl NCs can be deconvoluted into 2 peaks at 399.50 and 400.14 eV. The Cl 2p peak can be resolved into two peaks at 198.6 eV and 200.2 eV, corresponding to Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2, respectively. These peaks confirm the presence of NH4Cl flux in ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs ( Fig. 3 f). The N1 s peak at 399.50 eV corresponds to nitrogen in amine (–NH2) or imine (C=N) groups, typically found on the surface of CDs. The peak at 400.14 eV represents nitrogen in graphitic or pyrrolic forms, indicating nitrogen incorporation into the carbon framework. The N1 s band can be deconvoluted into two peaks (Fig. 3 g), which correspond to amino N (N-H, 399.1 eV) and (C3- N, 400.2 eV) [46]. These XPS peaks provide valuable information about the chemical composition and bonding environment of the synthesized materials. The C 1 s peak observed in CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ /NH4Cl NCs can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 284.4 eV and 288 eV. The peak at 284.4 eV corresponds to C-C, while the peak at 288 eV corresponds to O-C=O components, as shown in Fig. 3 h [ 47 ].

The structure and composition of ZAO:7Cr3+, ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs, CDs, CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs are characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy shown in Fig. S4. The FT-IR spectra display bands near 3398 cm-1 and 1615 cm-1. The broad band around 3398 cm-1 is attributed to OH group stretching vibrations, while the weak band around 1615 cm-1 is associated with the deformative vibration of water molecules. Moreover, spectral features within the 1000 to 400 cm⁻¹ range are associated with the vibrational modes of Al-O bonds. Two well-defined bands at 668 cm-1 and 567 cm-1 are observed in the spectra, characteristic of spinel bands of ZAO. These bands are primarily related to vibrations of the lattice of octahedral AlO6 in ZAO, with no peaks related to tetrahedral AlO4 observed in the region of 950 to 750 cm-1. These bands are observed similarly in ZAO:7Cr3+ and ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs. The results confirm the formation of the normal spinel structure in ZAO:7Cr3+ and ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs, consistent with XRD investigations. This agreement between FT-IR and XRD results provides further confirmation of the crystalline structure of the synthesized materials. [48]. Fig. S4 illustrates that CDs exhibit main absorption peaks attributed to OH/NH stretching vibrations, observed in the range from 3454 to 3344 cm⁻¹. These functional groups are responsible for the hydrophilic nature and stability of CDs. Moreover, a minor peak at 2783 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the C-H bond. The peaks observed at 1708 and 1598 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of O-C=O and C=C bonds, respectively. Furthermore, the stretching vibration of the CH/CN band is observed at 1368 cm−1. The stretching band of the amine H-N bond appears at 1156 cm−1. Additionally, IR bands ranging from 1050 to 570 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of C-O, CH2, and C-N, respectively. The FT-IR spectra of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ /NH4Cl NCs show a band at 3454 cm-1 related to OH/NH stretching vibration. The broad band from 1703 to 1590 cm-1 corresponds to O-C=O and C=C stretching vibrations of CDs. The band at 761 cm-1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C-O. These observations confirm the effective incorporation of CDs into ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs, further enhancing their functional properties [48,49].

Morphology significantly influences the performance of NPs. FE-SEM images in Fig. 4
(a & b) depict the irregular morphology, serious agglomeration, and poor crystallinity of ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs prepared without flux, leading to indeterminate morphology and aggregations. The absence of flux results in poor crystallization, adversely affecting luminescence intensity. In contrast, Fig. 4 (c–e) displays SEM images of phosphor samples prepared with different fluxes (NaCl, KCl, NH4Cl). While NaCl and KCl fluxes yield particle sizes and morphologies similar to those without flux, the sample with NH4Cl flux exhibits highly uniform powder particle shapes, indicating excellent crystallinity and enhanced luminescence intensity [50]. During calcination, NH4Cl flux melts into a liquid phase, eliminating direct solid–solid grain boundaries and promoting particle formation with a smooth surface. The improved crystallinity due to NH4Cl can be attributed to its reaction with metal nitrate, forming ammonium nitrate. NH4Cl plays a dual role as both a combustible material and an oxidizing agent. This enhances the exothermic nature of the redox reaction during synthesis and provides a molten medium for mixing fuel and oxidant, thereby improving crystallinity [23]. In contrast, NaCl and KCl-assisted samples exhibit low solubility and high melting points, resulting in minimal changes in crystallinity compared to NH4Cl. The presence of NH4Cl enhances ionic diffusion and increases particle crystallization, indicating significant surface modification of ZAO NPs by NH4Cl [51].

As shown in Fig. 4 f, the CDs are smaller in size. To explore the effect of CDs on morphology, the SEM image of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs is presented in Fig. 4 g. Similar morphology of CDs aggregation on NPs can be observed in Fig. 4 h, the SEM image of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs. To further understand the embedding of CDs on ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs, the enlarged SEM image is given in Fig. 4 i. These results indicate the improved crystallinity of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs, which is also supported by the XRD result. Moreover, CDs act as surface modifiers, further enhancing the properties of the NPs [52].

TEM images in Fig. 5
a show the irregular morphology and low crystallinity of ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs synthesized without flux, resulting in an undefined morphology. The particles are found to have an average size of ∼17.23 nm, as shown in Fig. 5 a1 . The lack of flux leads to inadequate crystallization, which negatively impacts luminescence intensity. In contrast, Fig. 5 b presents TEM images of phosphor samples prepared using NH4Cl fluxes. The sample treated with NH4Cl flux shows uniformly shaped powder particles, suggesting superior crystallinity and increased luminescence intensity [53]. NH4Cl serves a dual purpose as both a combustible material and an oxidizing agent, enhancing the crystallinity of NPs. As depicted in Fig. 5 c, the CDs are smaller in size, with an average particle size of ∼ 5.75 nm (Fig. 5 c 1). Fig. 5 d shows the TEM image of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs, aimed at investigating the influence of CDs on morphology. The average particle size of the NPs in CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs ranges from ∼14 to 18 nm. Specifically, the embedded CDs have an average particle size of ∼5.06 nm (Fig. 5 d1 ). Fig. 5 e shows the TEM image of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs, aimed at investigating the influence of CDs and fluxes on morphology. The average particle size of the NPs in CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs ranges from ∼14 to 19 nm. Specifically, the embedded CDs have an average particle size of ∼5.56 nm (Fig. 5 e1 ).

These findings highlight the enhanced crystallinity of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs, which is corroborated by XRD analysis. Additionally, CDs function as surface modifiers, further improving the characteristics of the NPs [54]. HR-TEM is the most effective and direct technique for examining the number of layers at the microscopic level. Fig. 5 f shows the HR-TEM image of optimized NPs. However, HRTEM images are successfully captured from fairly thicker areas and processed using GATAN software to obtain FFTs (Fig. 5 f1 ). By analysing the line profile of these patterns, we determined a d-spacing of 0.28 nm, corresponding to the (220) plane of ZAO:7Cr3+, which aligns closely with the XRD findings. The crystal lattice spacing of ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs is confirmed to be 0.28 nm, corresponding to the (220) lattice plane of ZAO:7Cr3+, as shown in Fig. 5 (g & g1). Additionally, a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, indicative of the graphitic carbon (110) plane, is observed (Fig. 5 (h & h1)). To further verify the presence of CDs on the surface of the NPs, HR-TEM images of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ /NH4Cl NCs are displayed in Fig. 5 i and j. Fig. 5 i1 & j1 shows a crystal lattice spacing of 0.28 nm, representing the ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs. Fig. 5 i2 & j2 shows a crystal lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, suggesting that CDs may be predominantly doped onto the surface of ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs.

Fig. 6
a displays the PLE spectra of ZAO:Cr3+ NPs. The electrons of Cr3+ are responsible for the detection of two excitation bands, centered at ∼ 408 and 530 nm, respectively, for the 4A2(4F) →4T1(4F) transition (ν2) and 4A2(4F) → 4T2(4F) transition (ν1). A shoulder peak can be seen in the ν2 transition band, and two Gaussian peaks at 390 and 413 nm fit this excitation band nicely. Due to spin-orbit coupling at the Al3+ center combined with trigonal distortion, these two fitted peaks are expected to correspond to the fine splitting of the excited 4T1 state into 4T1 (
t 2 2
e
1
) and 4T1(
t 1 2

1
) [55,56]. The material shows an asymmetric broadband emission between 600 and 750 nm when excited at 530 nm. The recorded PL emission spectra of ZAO:7Cr3+ stimulated at 530 nm are shown in Fig. 6 b. The data indicates the presence of four distinct sets of emission peaks. These are located at 667, 676, and 679 nm in the first set, 688, 690, and 698 nm in the second set (referred to as R1, R2, and N lines, respectively), 709, 718, and 723 nm in the third set, and 732 and 740 nm in the fourth set. These peaks arise due to the presence of Cr3+ in octahedral symmetry. The second pair of zero-phonon lines (ZPL) at 688 and 690 nm are attributed to the characteristic transitions (2Eg → 4A2 g) of the R1 and R2 lines of Cr3+ ions. The sharp emissions at 698 nm correspond to the N lines of Cr3+ ions interaction. These N lines and R lines, respectively, are the outcome of the 2Eg level splitting caused by spin-orbit coupling, strong crystal field, and crystal defect disturbance. The first and third groups of multi-phonon sidebands (PSB), located on either side of the zero-phonon line (ZPL) in the spectrum, correspond to the anti-Stokes and Stokes phonon sidebands [57,58].

The crystal structure of as synthesised NPs has a close relationship with the PL characteristics of Cr3+. Cr3+ can be found in a weak or strong crystal field by calculating the ratio of crystal field intensity (Dq) to electron repulsion parameter B. A weak crystal field is present for Cr3+ if Dq/B <2.3. These energy levels correlate to two excitation bands in the ZAO:7Cr3+ spectra: one at 408 nm (4A2g→4T1g, 24,509 cm-1), and the other at 530 nm (4A2g→4T2g, 14,534 cm-1). It is determined that the Cr3+ crystal field strength in this sample is 3.58. This suggests that Cr3+ is occupying the [AlO6] octahedra and is undergoing narrow-band emission-related strong crystal field. The junction of the 2Eg and 4T2g(F) energy levels is located at approximately, Dq/B = 3.58. The outcome shows that Cr is located in a strong ligand field. Meanwhile, it is suggested by the dominance of emission spectrums the 2Eg → 4A2g transition. Furthermore, as Table S3 [60–65] illustrates, we contrasted the particular crystal field properties of ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs with those of other Cr3+ doped NPs.

The luminescence intensity of ZAO:Cr3+ (1–11 mol%) NPs shows a link with the Cr3+ doping concentration, as Fig. 6 c illustrates. At a doping ion concentration of 7 mol%, the highest luminescence intensity is seen. Nevertheless, the luminescence intensity begins to decline beyond this concentration. At increasing doping concentrations, the near proximity of Cr3+ ions cause energy transfer loss, which lowers luminescence intensity and is the reason for this reduction. As per the Dexter theory, the critical distance (Rc) is determined using Equation [66]:
(15)
R c
= 2
(
3 V
4 π

)
1 3

N is the number of unit crystal cells in the formula, V is the volume of individual cells, and Xc is the critical concentration of dopant ions. In ZAO, V is equivalent to 542 Å³, N is equivalent to 6, and Xc of Cr3+ is 0.07. Therefore, it is calculated that the ZAO:7Cr3+ ions have a Rc of roughly 13.39 Å. Concentration quenching is attributed more to multipole-multipole interactions rather than exchange interactions between Cr3+ ions, primarily because the computed Rc value exceeds the critical distance required for exchange action (5 Å). These interactions are associated with particular values of Q (Q = 6, 8, and 10). The relationship between emission intensity (I) and doping ion concentration (x) is determined by the formula [67]:

The ZAO:Cr3+ NPs linear connection between log(I/x) and log(x) is seen in Fig. 6 d. The slope of the linear fitting is –1.68. The d-d interaction within the ZAO:Cr3+ NPs is most likely responsible for the concentration quenching process, as indicated by the predicted Q value of 5.04, which is very near to 6.

The luminescence intensity of ZAO:7Cr3+ with chloride fluxes is higher than that of the sample without fluxes, as shown in Fig. 6 e. The emission spectra confirm the notable raise in luminescence intensity seen in ZAO:7Cr3+ samples containing NaCl, KCl, and NH4Cl. The sample with NH4Cl showed the greatest rise in luminescence intensity, reaching 3.82 folds that of the sample without a flux. Furthermore, as Fig. 6 f illustrates, the samples with NaCl and KCl fluxes also had increases of 1.21 and 1.99 folds, respectively. This enhancement is explained by the low melting point of NH4Cl (340 °C) and the early entry of NH4Cl into the liquid phase, paving the way for effective interaction between the reaction components throughout the sintering process [68].

At room temperature, PL spectra are measured to investigate the luminescence properties of CDs. The excitation spectrum monitoring at 538 nm is shown in Fig. 7
a. The π-π* and n-π* transitions of functional groups in CDs are responsible for two peaks at 238 and 404 nm, respectively [69]. The maximal emission position under 404 nm light excitation is found at 538 nm, as shown in Fig. 7 a.

The PLE spectrum intensity of ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs is significantly lower than that of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs as displayed in Fig. 7 b. The PLE spectrum of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs monitored at 688 nm is composed of many peaks such as 277, 403 and 530 nm. Apart from the excitation peak of Cr3+ at the corresponding wavelength, there are distinct broad bands at 403 nm, attributed to π-π* and n-π* transitions of functional groups in CDs. This results powerfully prove the energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+ in CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs. Furthermore, the PL spectra of ZAO:7Cr3+ and CDs(1, 3, 5, 7 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ nanomaterials under 530 nm excitation are shown in Fig. S4a. CDs (5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs had the maximum PL emission. The PL spectra of the ZAO:7Cr3+ and CDs (5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ nanomaterials under 530 nm excitation are shown in Fig. 7 c. When ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs are incorporated with CDs, there is an impressive 11.23-fold increase in the emission intensity. The change in CDs from solution to composite powder could be the cause of this improvement. Furthermore, the luminescence intensity of the CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs is significantly boosted, attributable to the energy transfer between CDs and Cr3+ ions. To further confirm the existence of CDs in CDs (5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs, PL emission spectra of ZAO:7Cr3+ and CDs (5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs are studied upon CDs excitation (403 nm) as shown in Fig. S4b. For CDs (5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs, as expected, it shows both CDs centered broad emission and characteristic sharp emissions of Cr3+.The broad emission band of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs is consistent with that of CDs.

Fig. 7 d presents the energy transfer and luminescence processes between ZAO:7Cr3+ and CDs in a clear and concise manner. First, the electrons are moved from the C π to the C π* energy level when the CDs absorb UV light. Then, using the C π*-N π* and N π*-O π* processes, the electrons are relaxed to the lowest LUMO energy level, when energy is transferred to Cr3+ ions and electrons transit to the C π energy level by green emission. In the meantime, the emission is caused by the Cr3+ ions absorbing the photon energy and producing the transition from 4A2g to 4T1g and 4T2g energy levels. Furthermore, with CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs, the excited energy electrons at the 4T1g and 4T2g levels of Cr3+ are collected by CDs and then redirected to the 4Eg energy level via the FRET mechanism. As a result, there are more electrons at the Cr3+ 4Eg level. We measured the PL spectrum of CDs and the PLE spectrum of ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs in order to confirm the energy transfer. The fact that the excitation peaks of Cr3+ ions at 530 nm overlap with the strong emission band of CDs at 450–700 nm, as seen in Fig. 7 e, further supports the idea that energy is transferred from CDs to Cr3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative studies of ZAO:7Cr3+, CDs(5wt%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ nc and CDs(5wt%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/ NaCL/KCL/NH4Cl/ as modifiers
ce:section.ce:para: The PL study revealed that among chloride fluxes, NH4Cl exerted the most significant impact, increasing the PL intensity of ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs by 3.82 times. Additionally, CDs demonstrated their effectiveness as the most potent modifiers, enhancing the PL intensity by 11.23 times. In the case of addition of different fluxes (NaCl, KCl and NH4Cl) added in to CDs(5wt%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NC, the PL intensity enhances 13, 14 and 17 % respectively (Fig. 7f & g). The enhancement of PL intensity upon addition CDs and flux in to phosphor can be explained based on (i) CDs have a high absorption cross-section, allowing them to efficiently absorb a broad spectrum of light. This efficient absorption increases the excitation of the phosphor material, leading to enhanced emission. In contrast, fluxes mainly improve the crystallinity and remove impurities in the phosphor but do not contribute directly to light absorption or emission (ii) CDs can act as energy transfer agents. When incorporated into a phosphor matrix, they can absorb energy and transfer it to the phosphor's luminescent centers. This energy transfer process significantly boosts the PL intensity by providing additional excitation energy to the phosphor, enhancing its luminescent efficiency beyond what is achievable with fluxes alone (iii) the surface chemistry of CDs, which often includes functional groups, can passivate defects and minimize non-radiative recombination pathways. This results in fewer energy losses and enhances the PL intensity. The functional groups can also be tailored to optimize the interaction with the phosphor matrix, further improving the overall luminescence properties (iv) CDs reduce non-radiative recombination losses by creating a more favorable electronic environment for radiative transitions. Fluxes, on the other hand, primarily improve crystallinity and reduce defects in the bulk material, which also enhances PL but not to the extent that CDs can achieve through both structural and electronic modifications (v) the unique electronic structure of CDs allows for narrow emission spectra with high color purity [70]. This is beneficial for applications requiring specific color outputs and contributes to higher overall PL intensity. Fluxes do not significantly affect the emission spectra in this manner. The present results clearly indicate that PL intensity increase in CDs@ZAO:7Cr3+ NC compared to ZAO:7Cr3+/NaCl/KCl/NH4Cl NC is due to the combined effects of enhanced light absorption, efficient energy transfer, surface passivation, reduced non-radiative losses, and narrow emission spectra. These properties make CDs highly effective in boosting the luminescent properties of phosphor materials. Table 2 displays a comparison of the enhanced PL characteristics of various modified phosphor with present work [71–77,22].

The CIE, CCT and CP of ZAO:Cr3+(1–11 mol %), ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl/NaCl/KCl NCs and CDs(1–7 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs are studied and shown in Fig. 7h & i. By using standard relations [78,79] CP and CCT values are estimated and shown in Table S4. As can be seen from results obtained in table, at low Cr³⁺ concentrations (1–5 mol %), the phosphor might exhibit a narrow emission spectrum, often dominated by specific transitions such as the R-line emission in the red region. The CIE coordinates would reflect this limited emission, typically resulting in a colour concentrated around a specific point in the red or near-infrared region. However, for optimal concentration of Cr³⁺ ions (7 mol %) where the emission intensity is maximized, and the phosphor displays a rich colour purity (97.2 %). This is due to efficient energy transfer and minimized quenching effects at this concentration. Further, at high Cr³⁺ concentrations (9 & 11 mol %), the emission spectrum might broaden due to increased interactions between Cr³⁺ ions, leading to possible self-quenching which leads slight shift in CIE coordinates. The addition of fluxes affects the PL intensity and colour purity of NPs. Compared to NaCl and KCl, NH₄Cl results in higher PL intensity and colour purity (98.1 %) because it decomposes at relatively low temperatures into ammonia (NH₃) and hydrogen chloride (HCl), both of which are gases. This decomposition creates a highly reactive environment during phosphor synthesis, which can improve crystallinity and enhance the emission properties of the phosphor.

Additionally, CDs added phosphor show higher PL intensity and colour purity compared to fluxes added phosphor because they possess a high absorption cross-section and can efficiently absorb light, resulting in enhanced excitation. Whereas both CDs and flux added NPs shows a maximum PL intensity compared to other composities. The unique electronic structure of CDs enables strong PL with narrow emission spectra, which enhances colour purity (99.8 %) with a CCT value 2322 K. Additionally, CDs often have surface functional groups that can passivate defects and minimize non-radiative recombination pathways, thereby increasing PL intensity. The surface chemistry of CDs can be adjusted to improve colour purity by reducing the broadening of the emission spectrum. CDs can also efficiently transfer energy to the surrounding phosphor matrix, enhancing overall luminescence. This effective energy transfer significantly boosts PL intensity, unlike fluxes, which mainly enhance crystallinity and remove impurities but do not directly contribute to the luminescence process [80].

In general, it is widely acknowledged that I
QE plays a pivotal role in determining the suitability of a phosphor for use in w-LED devices. Under 365 nm excitation, the I
QE of ZAO:7Cr3+, ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl, and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+phosphor is investigated, and the I
QE value is determined by the following equation [81]:
(17)
I
Q E
=
∫
L S
∫
E R
− ∫
E S

Here, LS denotes the emission spectrum of the samples, while ES represents the spectrum of the excitation light with the sample, and ER signifies the spectrum of the excitation light with only the BaSO4 reference. The I
QE value for the ZAO:7Cr3+, ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl, CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4CL phosphor is calculated as 67.3, 70.4, 87.5 and 93.4 % as shown in Fig. 8 (a-d). IQE of Cr3+ doped NPs is given in Table S5 [82–92]. From these results we can say that CDs enhance I
QE of NPs more compared to NH4Cl.

The decay lifetime of NPs in their prepared state is computed. ZAO:7Cr3+, ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl, and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs (λexc i = 530 nm) of R1 lines decay curves are displayed in Fig. 8(e,f). To estimate the lifetime value using a single exponential function, the decay of a signal, such as luminescence or fluorescence, is typically modelled with the following formula: [93]:
(18)
I
( t )
=
I 0
e x p
( − τ / t )

Where, I(t) is the intensity of the signal at time t, I0​ is the initial intensity of the signal, τ is the lifetime of the decay. The estimated lifetime values of ZAO:7Cr3+and ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl are found to be 5.90 ms and 6.59 ms respectively. The results show that the lifetime value is 1.1 times greater in ZAO:7Cr³⁺/NH₄Cl compared to ZAO:7Cr³⁺, indicating that the flux has a significant effect on the lifetime. In addition, the lifetime also depends on type and concentration of the flux can influence the crystal growth of the NPs and defect density. Different fluxes can impact the energy transfer mechanisms and non-radiative pathways within the phosphor. Also, fluxes often improve the crystallinity of the phosphor by promoting better crystal formation and reducing defects. Enhanced crystallinity typically results in longer lifetimes due to fewer non-radiative decay sites. Flux can modify the energy transfer efficiency between the dopant ions and the host lattice. Changes in energy transfer pathways can either increase or decrease the lifetime of the phosphor [94].

Fig. 8g & h shows the decay curve of CDs(5 wt. %)@ ZAO:7Cr3+ and CDs(5 wt. %)@ ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs excited under 530 nm which is well fitted to double exponential function as shown below [95]:

where A1, A2 are constants, I(t) is the emission intensity at time t, and τ1, τ2 are the double exponential decay time components. The average lifetime of a phosphor or decay process, when multiple exponential components are present, can be calculated using a weighted average of the individual lifetimes. For a system with multiple exponential decay components, the average lifetime (τavg) is given by [96]:

where, A1 and A2 are the amplitudes of the exponential decay components, τ1​ and τ2​ are the respective lifetimes of these components. The τavg values of ZAO:7Cr3+, CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+NCs and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs are found to be 5.90 ms 8.33 ms and 8.83 ms respectively. The energy transfer efficiency is calculated using lifetime values:

When energy transfer affects the emission lifetime, estimating the efficiency based on the change in lifetime values is straightforward. The lifetime efficiency (η) can be calculated using the following formula [97]:
(21)

Where, τ0 is the initial lifetime (before energy transfer) value of CDs (7.81 ns), τtrans ​ is the lifetime after energy transfer of NCs (2.14 ns) and (1.71 ns). The estimated lifetime efficiency value of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+NCs and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs are found to be 0.726 and 0.781 is close to that of the CDs@MgWO₄:Eu³⁺ (2.31 ns) and CDs@MgWO₄:Tb³⁺ (2.25 ns) NCs [71].

Fig. 8i,j & k show the luminescent decay curves for CDs, CDs@ZAO:7Cr³⁺ and CDs@ZAO:7Cr³⁺/NH4Cl NCs respectively. The decay curve for CDs fits a single exponential function with a lifetime of approximately 7.25 ns (Fig. 8g). For CDs@ZAO:7Cr³⁺ NC, the decay curve is well-fitted by a double exponential function (Fig. 8h). This double exponential decay behaviour is typically seen in luminescent materials where energy transfer occurs. The average lifetime of NCs is lower than that of pure CDs, suggesting that energy transfer between CDs and the ZAO:7Cr³⁺ NPs is occurring.

To explore the temperature sensing capabilities of ZAO:Cr3+ NPs, emission spectra are measured at various temperatures. Fig. 9
(a–d) illustrate the temperature-dependent photoluminescence (TDPL) spectra for ZAO:7Cr3+, ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl, CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs, monitored at excitation wavelengths of 530 nm. In the synthesized materials, all emission peaks exhibited a decrease in luminous intensity with increasing temperature from 300 to 480 K. However, with the introduction of flux (NH4Cl) and CDs into the ZAO lattice, the intensity of the R1 line remained stable with increasing temperature, especially in ZAO crystals doped with CDs. This indicates that the R1 line is suitable as a reference signal for the ratio metric optical thermometer. Comparing the relative intensity of the R1 line (688 nm) of ZAO:7Cr3+, ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl, CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs with temperature, as shown in Fig. 9e. At 420 K, CDs (5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs exhibit the highest thermal stability at 92.43 %, compared to CDs incorporated into ZAO:7Cr3+, which show a stability of 91.04 %. In contrast, ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl nanoparticles demonstrate a substantial thermal stability of 87.53 %. The work by N. Zhang et al. suggests that the incorporation of fluxes into yielded NPs results in excellent thermal stability. The literature review on some Cr3+ doped NPs, as provided in Table 3 [97–104], indicates that CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs demonstrate excellent thermal stability compared to ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs.

As depicted in Fig. 9 (a–d), the emission peak and profile remain unchanged with increasing temperature, indicating stability in the emission characteristics. However, the emission intensity gradually decreases due to the thermal quenching (TQ) effect. Despite this, the extent of TQ is not severe, and even at 423 K, the integral intensity retains above 91.04 % of that at 298 K. This indicates that the sample has good thermal stability and satisfies the necessary practical standards for w-LED applications. The Struck and Fonger theory can be used to explain the link between temperature and the emission integral intensity of the Cr3+ R1 line [105]:
(22)
I t
=
I 0
1 + c e x p
(
−
E a
k T
)

The thermal quenching of PL intensity is described by the following equation, where It and I0 represent the PL intensity measured at temperature T and the initial temperature (300 K), respectively. Ea denotes the activation energy of the excited state, A is a constant, and k is the Boltzmann constant (1.38 × 10−23J/K). As in Fig. 9f, Ea is obtained as 0.245 (ZAO:7Cr3+), 0.271 eV (ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl), 0.302 eV (CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+NCs) and, 0.354 eV (CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs) which is higher than the so far reported NPs as shown in Table 3. The configuration coordinate diagram (CCD) depicted in Fig. 9 g illustrates the mechanism by which the excited electron transitions to the higher 4T2 state with increasing temperature, overcoming an energy barrier. The Ea between the R1 of the 2Eg→ 4A2g transition and the crossing point between the 2Eg and 4T2g potential curves is determined to be 0.354 eV. This value signifies a reasonable energy gap for the thermal activation of electrons from the 2Eg state to the 4T2g state as temperature increases. Consequently, it confirms that CDs serve as superior modifiers, enhancing the thermal stability compared to NH4Cl for ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs. A higher activation energy suggests that the NPs are less susceptible to thermal excitation and energy loss [106]. NPs exhibiting higher Ea values tend to be more thermally stable, rendering them suitable for w-LED applications. Thus, the incorporation of CDs into ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs significantly enhances thermal stability compared to NH4Cl flux.

The trend depicted in Fig. 9(a–d) highlights a notable decrease in intensity and increase in the FWHM as the temperature rises. This indicates that the FWHM of the emission spectra is sensitive to temperature variations, presenting an advantage for the development of FWHM based thermometers (Fig. 9h). Moreover, the emission band exhibits an asymmetric broadening phenomenon with rising temperatures, corresponding to the multiple distinct Cr3+ luminescence sites in the ZAO host [107]. To ensure accurate results when using optical thermometry, it's important to consider the Sr value. This value indicates the level of sensitivity of the thermometer to temperature changes and shows how the thermometric parameter changes per Kelvin. With the help of the below-defined formula, Sr can be calculated [108]:
(23)
S r

According to the formula mentioned above, the Sr of all samples are calculated, and the results are presented in Fig. 9i–l. The CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+NCs, with 530 nm excitation wavelength has better thermometry performance. Comparing Sr is a common method for assessing the thermometry performance of different thermometers [109]. The maximum Sr of ZAO:7Cr3+, ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs are estimated to be 6.74 × 10–2, 5.93 × 10–2, 2.09 × 10–2 and 0.89 × 10–2 % K-1 respectively. The maximum Sr is 6.74 × 10–2 % K-1 at 300 K, which is much larger than that ZAO:7Cr3+ modified with NH4CL (2.09 × 10–2 %K−1) as shown in Fig. 9i–l, confirming that the sensitivity of the sample modified with both CDs and NH4Cl fluxes. Table 4 [110–115] presents the thermometric properties of NPs. Compared to these thermal sensing systems, the CDs-incorporated ZAO:7Cr3+ system demonstrates superior temperature sensing behaviour.

To check the applicability of ZAO:7Cr3+, ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl, CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs for dactyloscopy the FPs of same donor are developed on AL. foil. To generate the sample LFPs, the FP powders are brushed smoothly onto aluminium foil. The lipid residues inside the LFP's ridges and the as-synthesised powders interact hydrophobically to form the FPs. Next, by subjecting the FP residues to a UV lamp with a long wavelength of 365 nm, fluorescent pictures of the LFP are developed. The LFPs visualized by ZAO:7Cr3+, ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl, CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs powders showing complete patterns of papillary ridges can be seen in Fig. 10
(a–d). The 3D interactive surface plot of developed FPs are extracted using ImageJ software [116] are shown in Fig. 10 (a1-d1). The distribution of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+NCs is more uniform than that of ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs, which indicates more detail of sharpness, which is in accordance with the experimental images. Whereas compared to CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+NCs, the CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs were emitting intense red colour for FP detection. Using ImageJ software, grayscale pixel values are retrieved along green lines to assess each powder's recognition efficiency. The photographs grayscale pixel values differed greatly from one another. The friction ridges and furrows could be clearly recognized in CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs in Fig. 10 d2 . Even though ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs (Fig. 10 b2 ) emitted the strong FL, FP images from ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs (Fig. 10 a2 ) showed lower resolution and poor-quality image compared to CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+NCs. Whereas the FPs developed using ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs are better compared to ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs. The presence of insufficient uniform grayscale pixel values leads to incorrect tolerances in the values of the grayscale beside ridges and grooves of FPs. However, Visualization by CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4ClNCs is more sensitive than other synthesised samples. Therefore, CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4ClNCs is selected for further studies investigating FL intensity, resolution and adhesiveness [117].

In order to explore the applicability of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NCs for dactyloscopy further, LFPs are created on various surfaces. The substrates are marble, leaf, and aluminium foil. High contrast and high-resolution clear pictures of LFPs are successfully created (Fig. S6). The type of substrates used has an impact on the as developed FPs. On all substrates, the FP pattern is sufficiently visible due to the vivid red colour of NCs. Resolution analysis of the obtained FP images is done using the pixel profile can be seen from Fig. S6(a1-c1). FPs on Al. foil show better sharpness, high homogeneity which is in accordance with pixel plot. Though there is non-uniformness in gray value of FPs on porous surface we can easily distinguish between ridges and furrows. From the 3D interactive plots of developed FPs we can observe distribution of NPs over ridges in Fig. S6(a2-c2). We can observe uniform distribution of NPs on LPs developed on both non-porous and semi-porous surface. Therefore, the development of LFPs using CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4ClNCs shows a high developing selectivity.

The thumbprint on the steel plate are examined for level I-III features in order to validate the FP information [118]. The extraction of primary and secondary level information of LFPs is made possible by the high quality of the fluorescent FP pictures on the non-porous substrate (Fig. 11
a). The general pattern of this sample is categorized as a right loop on thumb as the level I feature. We could clearly see level II feature, which are thought to be essential for personal identification and include bifurcations, ridge ends, enclosures, Hook, Islands, and dots. In addition to the contentious aspects mentioned above, the ridge width and furrow width (referred to as ridgeoscopy), as well as the size, shape, and position of the pores, note that only employing level II details is incapable of problematic FP comparison (FPs with low quality or spoof FPs), many researchers turn to explore the evidentiary power of level III details. Ridge width is another level III statistic related to ridges, typically measured in the range of 200–500 μm. As seen in Fig. 11 a1 , we can also see level III details using synthesised NCs. From ImageJ, the ridgeoscopic information of the chosen region of both FPs, including the ridge width and furrow width, is retrieved. Between 200 and 400 μm is found to be the ridge width. Pores are one of the several level III traits that have drawn the most notice. Because of the skin's elasticity and compression, a pore that experiences distortion stretches, changing in size, shape, and type. The inter-distance, however, stays roughly the same that is, within a tolerable, repeatable range. The repeatable pore inter-distance is the distance between the centre of one pore and the centre of the subsequent pore. The interpore spacing, determined with ImageJ software (Fig. 11 a2 ), indicates that the spacing between the pores varies from 300 to 600 μm.

Further the pore size is extracted from the FPs to study the applicability of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs for level III feature extraction. Pore size is calculated for consecutive pores ranges between 100 and 200 μm in the thumb print. Size of the pore is dependent on changes in the secretory activity of sweat pores. Pores exist in a variety of shapes including round, oval, elliptical/oblong, triangular, rhomboid, and square. Pore shape is not a reproducible feature as, distortions caused due to pressure change the basic shape of pores. In thus study we observed pores in circle, triangle, rectangle, oval, square shape in the thumbprint. The recent studies [119], suggest that round/circular and oval/elliptical are the most common pore shapes whereas, rhomboid and rectangular pores are less common. Each individual's thumbprint, whether from the right or left thumb, is unique. However, the differences between the thumbprints on the right and left hands do not hold any inherent biological significance. They are merely a product of the individual's genetic composition and environmental factors encountered during development [120]. To understand the applicability of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs on FPs the 3D interactive plot of the complete FPs and minutia sweat pore is extracted from ImageJ displayed in Fig. 11 (a3, & a4). The 3D interactive plot of the selected sweat pore shows the uniform distribution of NCs over the edges of the sweat pore. We can conclude that CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs can outline FPs clearly. A clean, high-contrast FP image and the detail of level 3 sweat pores can be observed with dusting of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs on LFPs.

We are also aging LFPs that have been dusted with CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs on aluminium foil for different times. LFPs can be days or years old, and they eventually become dry and brittle when they are frequently discovered at crime scenes. This presents a problem for existing FP development methods, such as superglue fuming or powder dusting, which are susceptible to moisture and could contaminate the evidence. We suggest that CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs provide a safe way to examine older FPs because they are typically more viscous than newly deposited FPs. We develop aged FPs on aluminium surfaces that have been exposed to air for predefined periods of time (1 to 60 days) and use NCs to visually improve them in order to test our theory which is depicted in Fig. S7a. The FP's ridges and furrows may still be easily identified using a pixel profile, as shown in Fig. S7a1 , even after aging for up to 60 days. The ridges of the FP display high gray values, while the furrows exhibit low gray values. This suggests that ridges are where the powder sticks to more easily than furrows. These results imply that our CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs are useful for recognizing aged FPs, potentially providing contradiction-free person identification [121].

Thermal treatment for the developed FPs is conducted to check the thermal stability of FPs. The experiment is designed as heating to 50, 100 and 150 °C and cooling to room temperature. The photographs of FPs after treatment are depicted in Fig. S7b. Even after 100 °C temperature treatment the ridges are visible clearly but there is loss of intensity (gray value) which can observed from Fig. S7b1 . This validates the use of synthesised CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs for FPs visualization. To check stability of FPs they are treated with water for different time (Fig. S7c). This is validated using the 3D pixel plot for FPs treated with various solvents over the selected area. There is a gray scale variation observed in Fig. S7c1, still we can differentiate between the ridges and furrows. This demonstrates the stability of FPs enhanced by NCs [122].

The SEM image of the LFPs of right thumb of donor is taken to further deeper studies on application of CNs for FPs visualization (Fig. S8). The level II features scar, enclosure, hook, island, ridge end and bifurcation are observed in Fig. S8a. Level III details are the FP's minute details, which mostly consist of the ridge edges shape and the ridge and pores size. Using a SEM, level 3 microscopic features of developed FPs are examined. At high magnification, we observed the feature around sweat pores (Fig. S8b). The interpore spacing, size, area, and shape of sweat pores on a FP can be clearly observed as displayed in Fig. S8b1–5 respectively. The extracted Poroscopic details are consistent with the earlier results in Fig. 11. The inter pore spacing in Fig. S8b1 is found to be in range 250 −725 µm. The size of the pore is ranging between 142 −240 µm as shown in Fig. S8b2 . The area of the pore is calculated from the SEM image accurately and displayed in Fig. S8b3 . The pore is found to be OS (one side open), BS (both side open), and C (closed) pores. Different shaped pores like circle, triangle, square and open pore are observed separately in Fig. S8b5 . The pixel plot of selected region in plot as Fig. S8b6 . We can even observe the sweat pores in pixel profile along with ridges and furrows.

We customized AC devices using the brush mode in order to test their viability for security applications [123]. The substrate must be prepared by repeatedly washing in ethanol and water, then drying at room temperature before drawing. Patterns created using CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs ink are colourless in the daylight, but they can produce intense red FL when exposed to 365 nm UV light, as Fig. 12
a clearly illustrates. The customized patterns are then brush-deposited onto the surfaces of several substrates, including glass, steel, craft paper, and plastic, to test the viability of the as-prepared ink. Notably, in daylight, no printed pattern has been seen on the surfaces of craft paper, plastic, steel, or glass. AC devices on the above substrates display strong red FL and are visible under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light, indicating that the CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs possesses excellent practicability on the various substrates [124,125].

To check the practicability of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs ink for AC applications the developed devices are treated with different solvents. Fig. 12 b shows the various pH solvent treatment of AC devices. Solvents like water, HCl and NaOH with different pH value 7, 3 and 10 are prepared and the AC tags are dipped in the solvents for 15 min. AC tags are characterized to obtain their FL intensities and the plot is displayed in Fig. 12 b1 . There is slight intensity drop observed for water treatment. Whereas for HCl there observed a intensity drop of 92.5 %. This is sufficient for applicability of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4ClNCs for AC applications. Additionally, the inks FL stability is assessed using UV treatment over varying time intervals (Fig. 12 c). After maintaining a range of steady fluorescent intensities for 30 to 60 min, a 80.53 % decrease in intensity is seen (Fig. 12 c1 ). As a result, the AC tags displayed outstanding UV resistance. The long-term storage stability of NCs AC inks is evaluated using the AC pattern as a test specimen. The patterns are held under ambient conditions for 60 days, starting on day 1 (Fig. 12 d). As shown in Fig. 12 d1 , a sequence of steady FL intensities is sustained for 60 days, after which there is a minor decrease in intensity to 82.25 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application performance of the fabricated LEDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To demonstrate the feasibility of illuminating with the red phosphor, w-LEDs are prepared using ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl, CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4CL NCs, as shown in Fig. 13
(a, b &c). The w-LEDs fabricated with ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl, CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4CL NCs emit a brilliant white light with decent correlated color temperatures (CCT) of 6082 K, 5663 K and 5517 K respectively. The CIE chromaticity coordinates of the w-LEDs using ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl, CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs are (0.321, 0.325), (0.329, 0.331), and (0.332, 0.331) respectively, which are close to the equal energy point (0.333, 0.333), visually indicating the luminescent color and CIE position as shown in Fig. 13 d.

The CRI value serves as a significant parameter for evaluating w-LEDs. CRI is calculated and compared with commercial w-LEDs utilizing a blue chip and yellow Y3Al5O12:Ce3+ phosphor, as illustrated in Fig. 13 e. Various particular rendering indexes (R1–R14) are employed to assess the rendering performances of different colours. The w-LED fabricated using CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs exhibits a superior CRI (Ra = 94) compared to the w-LED fabricated using ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs (Ra = 86) and commercial w-LED (Ra = 80). Particularly for R9, representing red light, the value for CDs (R9 = 88) is significantly higher than that for NH4Cl (R9 = 59) modifier. The high CRI suggests that CDs serve as a superior modifier compared to NH4Cl for ZAO:7Cr3+ phosphor in w-LED fabrication. Further, spectral analyses using electroluminescence (EL) were used to assess the colour stability of the fabricated w-LED at different current levels. Fig. 13 e shows that the test current varied from 30 to 360 mA. The LED rapidly grew in emission intensity as the driving current rose. Furthermore, the wavelength of the emission bands and shape did not change [126]. The CIE diagram of the w-LED at different currents is shown in Fig. 13 f. The CIE colour coordinates of the w-LED are almost steady and are centered in the white light region. The manufactured w-LED has a luminous effectiveness of 16.95 lm/W. These findings show that the fabricated LED has outstanding luminous stability. Table 5 displays the test results for the manufactured w-LED at various currents, including CIE chromaticity coordinates, correlated colour temperature (CCT), and colour rendering index (Ra). The results show that the w-LED made with CDs@ZAO:7Cr³⁺/NH4Cl NCs has a great deal of potential for application in the lighting sector.

This study compares the effectiveness of various fluxes (NaCl, NH4Cl, KCl) and CDs as modifiers for ZAO:7Cr3+ nanoparticles were synthesized using solid-state reaction. Among the chloride fluxes, NH4Cl proved to be the most effective, increasing the PL intensity of ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs by 3.82 folds. CDs emerged as the most effective modifier, boosting the PL intensity by 11.23 folds. When combined with NH4Cl, the NCs achieved a maximum PL enhancement of 17.25 folds. Additionally, CDs enhanced CP to 99.8 %, resulting in a CCT of 2322 K, making the NPs suitable for warm LED applications. The I
QE of ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl and CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ NPs were measured at 70.4 % and 87.5 %, respectively. In contrast, the CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs achieved the highest IQE of 93.4 %. NH4Cl enhanced the longevity of the NPs, while the longer decay times of CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+ indicate their superior glow characteristics. The NCs exhibited impressive thermal stability of 92.43 % at 420 K, with Ea values of 0.271 eV for ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl, 0.302 eV for CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+, and 0.354 eV for CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl. For optical thermometry, the CDs(5 wt.%)@ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NCs demonstrated excellent sensitivity, with a Sr of 6.74 × 102 %K-1 at 300 K. Furthermore, LFPs were distinctly visible on different substrates, and fluorescent ink is utilized to create customized AC devices. By incorporating CDs into ZAO:7Cr3+/NH4Cl NPs, a w-LED is produced with a CCT of 5517 K, a CIE coordinate of (0.332, 0.331) and Ra of 94. Overall, CDs surpass chloride fluxes as modifiers, leading to substantial enhancements in PL properties for applications in temperature sensing, fingerprint detection, AC devices and w-LEDs.
